title: Inside the Luddite Festival Harnessing Gen Z's Rage Against Big Tech
gist: A New York 'Summer of Ludd' festival channels growing Gen Z backlash against Big Tech, social media, and AI into offline community-building, reflecting a measurable rise in anti-tech sentiment, though experts doubt it will change actual usage behavior.

insights.claim: A 2025 Pew Research study found 48 percent of teen respondents in 2024 said social media has negative effects on people their age, up from 32 percent in 2022.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: A 16-point jump in two years in negative teen sentiment signals a durable shift in the youngest cohort's relationship with social platforms, a long-run engagement risk.
insights.quote: in 2024, 48 percent of teen respondents said social media has negative effects on people their age—up from 32 percent in 2022
insights.locator: The new Luddite movement has become heavily associated

insights.claim: Anti-tech sentiment is part of a broader trend: more people quitting dating apps for in-person gatherings like run clubs, AI-boosting commencement speakers being booed by graduates, and analog tech like cyberdecks gaining popularity.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: A cultural counter-current against digital-first products could pressure engagement metrics in dating apps and AI-branded consumer offerings.
insights.quote: More people are quitting dating apps, opting to meet people at in-person gatherings like run clubs. Commencement speakers who extol the virtues of AI have found themselves booed by college graduates.
insights.locator: This hostility toward the outsized role

insights.claim: An advanced-technology-transitions professor frames the historical Luddite movement as about labor rather than being anti-tech, and the modern term as positively describing pushback against tech eroding personal autonomy.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Reframing 'Luddite' as autonomy-defense rather than tech-rejection broadens the movement's potential appeal and mainstream relevance.
insights.quote: pushing back against the prevalence of tech and how it pulls away from their autonomy on multiple fronts

insights.claim: A web developer draws a parallel between original Luddites who had to rent big machines and today's creators dependent on SaaS and tools like Claude Code, arguing the fight is about building independent infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: The 'indie web' / infrastructure-ownership framing points to niche demand for de-platformed, self-hosted alternatives to dominant SaaS.
insights.quote: Most Luddites were technicians in some way, but they had to rent the infrastructure, the big machines. With things like Claude Code and SaaS, that's what we are seeing now

themes.theme: AI in workplaces as a driver

insights.claim: A former Big Tech security engineer quit his job because leadership encouraged non-technical people to write code with AI-assisted tools and push it to production, which he found deeply concerning.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Insider unease about AI-generated code shipped to production by non-engineers flags a governance/quality risk in AI-accelerated software development.
insights.quote: I quit my last job because our leadership was encouraging non-technical people to write code with AI-assisted tools and pushing them to production
insights.locator: An attendee and former Big Tech employee

insights.claim: The ASU professor doubts the movement will move the needle substantially, noting that even people who agree technologies are harmful rarely change how they live and keep using phones, social media, and AI.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: Attitude-behavior gap means sentiment backlash may not translate into usage declines, limiting near-term financial impact on platforms.
insights.quote: Even when people agree that they think these technologies are harmful, it rarely impacts the way they live their lives. They're still using their phones, social media, AI

insights.claim: Network effects entrench dominant platforms because leaving Facebook while friends remain cuts a person off from their friend circle, making individual exit costly.
insights.speaker: Vittoria Elliott
insights.so_what: The network-effect lock-in reaffirms why incumbent platform moats resist grassroots exit movements.
insights.quote: If you leave Facebook but all your friends are still on Facebook, you've just cut yourself off from your friend circle
